finding.contract_claim: document.getElementById("root") returns HTMLElement | null, so the non-null assertion `document.getElementById("root")!` passed to ReactDOM.createRoot will raise at runtime if the element is missing.
finding.observed_behavior: The code asserts non-null with `!` instead of a runtime check; the accompanying comment states the element is known to exist in index.html (standard React 18 bootstrapping pattern).
finding.triage_reasoning: The `!` non-null assertion is standard React 18 bootstrapping idiom. The comment on lines 5-6 explicitly documents the reasoning: 'we know the element exists in index.html.' This pattern appears in React's own documentation and virtually every React 18 project scaffold. The element's existence is guaranteed by the HTML template that the bundler serves — it's a build-time invariant, not a runtime unknown. Extracting a guard (`if (!el) throw ...`) would add noise without improving safety; the app cannot render without the root element anyway. Significance fails: closing this 'gap' doesn't improve the codebase meaningfully and the comment already documents the acknowledged trade-off.
